Long-term care hospital (LTCH) NA Patient was not discharged from an inpatient facility [Go to M1021] (M1005) Inpatient Discharge Date (Most Recent): 04/08/2026 UK Unknown Primary Reason for Home Health/Medical Necessity (POC Element): Diane Pastirik, a 65-year-old female, presents with multiple medical conditions including chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), congestive heart failure (CHF), coronary artery disease (CAD), hypertension (HTN), hyperlipidemia (HLD), and hyperthyroidism. She was recently hospitalized due to an exacerbation of COPD and CHF, resulting in acute on chronic respiratory failure and a non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI). Following her discharge from an inpatient rehabilitation facility, she has experienced a significant decline in her functional status, now requiring minimal assistance for daily activities such as bed mobility, transfers, and ambulation, often with the aid of a front-wheeled walker. The patient's current medical status necessitates skilled care, including nursing management for medication administration and monitoring of her complex medical needs, as well as physical and occupational therapy to address her mobility and self-care deficits. She requires ongoing assessment and management of her supplemental oxygen needs and other respiratory therapies, as well as pain management due to bilateral foot pain affecting her participation in rehabilitation activities. With her recent hospitalization, she has not only experienced functional decline but also faces challenges related to her chronic illnesses, which require careful management to prevent further complications. Given these factors, home health services are medically necessary to ensure she receives the appropriate skilled nursing care, therapy interventions, and assistance with activities of daily living (ADLs) in a familiar environment. The plan includes nursing for medication management and education, physical therapy focused on gait and balance training, and occupational therapy to support her in regaining independence with ADLs. The anticipated home health services will facilitate her recovery and help her achieve her rehabilitation goals, ultimately supporting her safe transition back to her previous living situation in a group home. The patient is considered homebound because of significant functional limitations and medical conditions that make it difficult and taxing to leave her residence. She presents with a complex medical history, including Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), Congestive Heart Failure (CHF), coronary artery disease (CAD), Hypertension (HTN), Hyperlipidemia (HLD), and hyperthyroidism. Following a recent hospitalization for an acute exacerbation of COPD and CHF, complicated by acute on chronic respiratory failure and a non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), she has experienced a marked decline in her functional status requiring home health services. Functionally, patient requires the aid of a front-wheeled walker for ambulation and needs minimal assistance with bed mobility, transfers, and ambulation. Objective findings indicate she requires partial/moderate assistance for most mobility tasks, including walking 10 feet, walking 50 feet with two turns, walking 150 feet, walking on uneven surfaces, and navigating steps (1, 4, and 12 steps). She also requires partial/moderate assistance with personal care activities such as bathing, upper and lower body dressing, and putting on/taking off footwear. Medically, she experiences dyspnea with minimal exertion, specifically when performing activities of daily living such as eating and agitation and is dependent on supplemental oxygen at 2 liters per minute. Her generalized weakness further restricts her ability to leave home safely. Leaving home requires a considerable and taxing effort for patient due to her severe chronic respiratory conditions and recent acute exacerbations, which induce dyspnea with minimal exertion. Her reliance on a front-wheeled walker, coupled with the need for partial/moderate assistance from another person for most mobility and self-care tasks, makes any departure from home physically strenuous and unsafe without substantial support. The reported generalized weakness and fatigue further contribute to the taxing nature of any attempted exit, necessitating extended rest periods. draftNarratives.label: Patient Summary / Clinical Narrative
draftNarratives.draft: Diane Pastirik is a 65-year-old female with a past medical history of CHF, COPD, CAD, HTN, HLD, and hyperthyroidism. She was admitted to the hospital with acute on chronic respiratory failure, pneumonia, CHF exacerbation, and NSTEMI. Following her hospitalization, she experienced a significant decline in functional status, now requiring minimal assistance for bed mobility, transfers, ambulation with a front-wheeled walker, and basic ADLs. She is now admitted to Encompass acute inpatient rehab for a comprehensive therapy program prior to safe discharge home.
